title: SEGMENT 1 — Opening and the central problem

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — Opening and the central problem
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.
Today and for the next two hours, we're going to talk about antibiotics — and specifically, we're going to talk about how they work.
The mechanisms.
Because this is a pharmacology course, not just a microbiology course, our central question isn't only "which bug does which drug kill.
" Our central question is: what is the molecular target, what does the drug do to that target, and why does interfering with that target either stop the bacterium from growing or kill it outright?

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can look at any antibiotic and tell me, one, what cellular process it attacks, two, the specific molecule it binds, and three, whether it's likely to be bacteriostatic or bactericidal and why — then we've succeeded.
Everything else, the clinical details, the dosing, the side effects, those hang off the mechanism.
Get the mechanism and the rest has somewhere to live in your memory.
So let me start with the single most important idea in this entire field.
It's a concept called selective toxicity.
Here's the problem we're trying to solve.
A bacterial infection is, by definition, bacterial cells growing inside a patient who is made of human cells.
We want to poison the bacteria.
We do not want to poison the patient.
And that's genuinely hard, because at the biochemical level, bacteria and human cells run on a lot of the same chemistry.
Both make proteins.
Both copy DNA.
Both run metabolism.
So if I just hand you a generic cell poison, it'll kill the bacteria, sure — and it'll kill the patient too.
That's not a drug.
That's a disinfectant.
Bleach is a wonderful antibacterial; we don't inject it.
The entire art of antibiotic pharmacology is finding the differences between a bacterial cell and a human cell, and then designing a molecule that exploits one of those differences.
A drug that hits something the bacterium has and absolutely needs, but that the human host either doesn't have at all, or has in a sufficiently different form that the drug ignores it.
That gap — the difference between the bacterial target and anything in us — is the source of the drug's safety margin.

When you hear later in this lecture that a particular antibiotic is unusually toxic, the underlying reason is almost always that its target in the bacterium is uncomfortably similar to something in our own cells.
When you hear that a drug is remarkably safe, it's usually because its target is something bacteria have that we simply do not.
So keep that frame in your head the whole time: every drug we discuss is an answer to the question, "what does a bacterium have that I don't, and how do I attack it?
